publications.title: Structural basis for the killing of human beta cells by CD8(+) T cells in type 1 diabetes.
publications.authors: Bulek AM, Cole DK, Skowera A, Dolton G, Gras S, Madura F, Fuller A, Miles JJ, Gostick E, Price DA, Drijfhout JW, Knight RR, Huang GC, Lissin N, Molloy PE, Wooldridge L, Jakobsen BK, Rossjohn J, Peakman M, Rizkallah PJ, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: This study determined the crystal structure of the autoreactive TCR 1E6 bound to HLA-A*0201 presenting a glucose-sensitive preproinsulin peptide, the antigen recognized by CD8+ T cells that kill insulin-producing beta cells in type 1 diabetes. The 1E6-pMHCI interaction exhibited unusually weak binding affinity despite rigid, lock-and-key engagement, driven by peptide-centric recognition through an aromatic-cap motif in the CDR3 loops with minimal contacts to the MHC class I surface itself. This structural mode—highly focused on peptide while making suboptimal overall TCR-pMHCI contacts—provides a mechanistic explanation for how an autoreactive TCR can escape negative selection in the thymus and subsequently mediate pathogenic recognition of self-antigen. The findings illuminate how weakly binding, peptide-focused TCRs can nonetheless drive autoimmune beta-cell destruction in type 1 diabetes, offering insights into the structural basis of autoimmune CD8+ T cell reactivity.

publications.title: Hotspot autoimmune T cell receptor binding underlies pathogen and insulin peptide cross-reactivity.
publications.authors: Cole DK, Bulek AM, Dolton G, Schauenberg AJ, Szomolay B, Rittase W, Trimby A, Jothikumar P, Fuller A, Skowera A, Rossjohn J, Zhu C, Miles JJ, Peakman M, Wooldridge L, Rizkallah PJ, Sewell AK.
publications.executive_summary: A human CD8+ T cell clone (1E6) derived from a type-1-diabetes patient was studied using high-resolution crystal structures of its T cell receptor bound to seven altered peptide ligands derived from preproinsulin and pathogenic peptides, all presented on MHC class I. The TCR achieves remarkable multiligand recognition—engaging over 1 million different peptides—through a conserved "hotspot" binding footprint that permits extensive sequence variation outside this critical contact region, with binding energetics shifting from enthalpy-dominated (weak, self-peptide) to entropy-dominated (strong, pathogen-derived peptide) interactions. This structural mechanism reveals how a single TCR can recognize both a weak self-antigen (insulin) and potent pathogen-derived peptides with altered affinities, explaining how molecular mimicry and cross-reactivity between microbial and self-antigens may break self-tolerance and precipitate autoimmune disease in type 1 diabetes.

publications.title: A "hotspot" for autoimmune T cells in type 1 diabetes.
publications.authors: Stadinski BD, Obst R, Huseby ES.
publications.executive_summary: This study examines an insulin-reactive TCR implicated in type 1 diabetes, revealing how a single TCR achieves cross-reactivity with pathogen-derived peptides by focusing binding interactions on a limited "hotspot" region within the peptide. The research demonstrates that autoimmune T cells recognize insulin through selective engagement with specific peptide residues, a principle that enables these cells to also respond to structurally distinct microbial antigens. This finding is significant for understanding type 1 diabetes pathogenesis, where molecular mimicry between pathogenic epitopes and self-insulin likely drives initial TCR activation of autoreactive clones. The results suggest that TCR specificity and cross-reactivity patterns can be predicted from standard protein-interaction principles, offering potential strategies for identifying which pathogens might trigger autoimmune responses and for engineering TCRs with controlled specificity profiles.
